title: Reducing the energy cost of your digital footprint
subtitle: How data centres and streaming consume electricity, and practical actions that actually reduce your share
excerpt: The global information and communications technology sector accounts for around 3.5 to 4 percent of global greenhouse gas emissions, a figure that is growing. Most of an individual's digital footprint comes from video streaming, cloud storage of photos and videos, and always-on devices. This guide covers the actual energy numbers, which actions have meaningful impact, and which popular advice is more symbolic than material.
sourceNotes: IEA Data Centres and Data Transmission Networks report (2022). Carbon Trust Digital Energy report. Mike Berners-Lee, How Bad Are Bananas? (Profile Books). Shift Project research on video streaming energy. Netflix transparency report on energy use per streaming hour.

glossaryTerms.definition: The ratio of total data centre energy to the energy used by the computing equipment alone. A PUE of 1.0 is theoretically perfect. Major cloud providers average 1.1 to 1.3. A PUE of 2.0 means half the energy is wasted on cooling and infrastructure.

The amount of data transmitted per second during video streaming. SD video uses 1 to 3 Mbps; HD video 5 to 8 Mbps; 4K video 15 to 25 Mbps. Higher bitrate means more data transferred and marginally more network and data centre energy per viewing hour.

content.text: The energy consumed by an hour of Netflix streaming on a TV connected via fibre broadband is around 0.036 kWh at the network and data centre (around 10 Wh at the device itself on a modern LED TV). For a household watching 3 hours per day, the network plus device total is roughly 4.5 to 5 kWh per day. This is real but comparable to running a refrigerator. The carbon implication depends almost entirely on the grid carbon intensity: streaming from a server running on renewable energy has a much lower carbon cost.

from 4K to HD reduces data transferred by 60 to 70 percent. The network energy saving per household per year at 3 hours per day is around 1.5 to 3 kWh: about 0.5 to 1 pound at current electricity rates. The saving is real but very small at household scale. At national scale, the aggregate matters.
Always-on devices on standby: a smart TV on standby uses 1 to 5 W continuously. At 5 W for 20 hours per day: 36.5 kWh per year. Switch off at the wall or use a smart plug on a timer.
Cloud photo backup: uploading hundreds of photos every day to multiple cloud services, and never pruning them, fills
storage that needs energy to keep running. Delete duplicates every quarter.

Email: a single stored email with a 5 MB attachment uses trivial energy. The advice to delete old emails is largely symbolic at individual scale. Unsubscribing from bulk marketing emails that you never read reduces inbox processing work but the carbon saving is tiny.
Cryptocurrency and NFTs: proof-of-work coins use far more energy per transaction than ordinary online activity. Cutting this out makes a real difference.
Extending device lifespan: the largest single action

Upgrading a laptop every 3 years versus every 6 years roughly doubles the embodied carbon cost per year of use. The embodied carbon in manufacturing a new laptop is 300 to 500 kg of CO2 equivalent. The energy to run a modern laptop at 30 to 50 W for 8 hours per day for 3 years is around 260 to 440 kWh, or 120 to 200 kg of CO2 at current UK grid intensity. Extending laptop life from 3 to 6 years halves the embodied carbon burden: this is worth 150 to 250 kg of CO2 per replacement deferred, which is more than a year of standby power savings.

attrs.symptom: Laptop running slowly, tempted to replace

content.text: Before replacing: add RAM if less than 8 GB (most laptops from 2015 onwards are upgradeable). Replace an HDD with an SSD if not already solid-state. These two upgrades typically restore a 5-year-old laptop to near-new performance at a total cost of 50 to 150 pounds versus 600 to 1,200 pounds for replacement.
